root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

products and dis/integrations

The Disintegration Theorems

Measure Disintegration Theorem. Let $(\calx,\mfm,\mu)$ and $(\caly,\mfn,\nu)$ be two $\sigma$-finite measure spaces. Suppose that $Q$ is a probability measure on $\calx \times \caly$ which has a density with respect to the product measure $\mu \times \nu$. If $R$ denotes the marginal distribution of $Q$ on $\caly$, then there is a stochastic transition kernel \begin{equation}\notag K: \mfm \times \caly \to \bbr, \quad (A,y) \mapsto K(A,y), \end{equation} that disintegrates $Q$ with respect to $R$, in the sense that \begin{equation}\label{gen-disint-eqn} Q(E) = \int_\caly K(E(y),y) \ R(\text{d}y) \end{equation} for all product-measurable subsets $E\subseteq \calx \times \caly$. In particular, if $A\in \mfm$ and $B\in \mfn$, we have \begin{equation}\label{special-disint-eqn} Q(A\times B) = \int_B K(A,y) \ R(\text{d}y). \end{equation}

We shall prove the theorem by constructing densities for the kernel $K$. To do so, first let $\varphi$ denote the density of $Q$ with respect to $\mu \times \nu$. By Fubini’s Theorem, we have

\begin{equation}\notag R(B) = Q(\calx \times B) = \int_{\calx \times B} \varphi(x,y) \ (\mu \times \nu)(\text{d}x,\text{d}y) = \int_B \left\{ \int_\calx \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \right\} \ \nu(\text{d}y), \end{equation}

which shows that $R$ has density

\begin{equation}\label{marginal-eqn} \pi(y) = \int_\calx \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \end{equation}

with respect to $\nu$. Now, for those $y\in \caly$ with $\pi(y)\neq 0$, define a function on $\calx$ by

\begin{equation}\label{cond-defn-eqn} x\mapsto f(x|y) \defeq \frac{\varphi(x,y)}{\pi(y)}. \end{equation}

It follows from \eqref{marginal-eqn} that $\int_\calx f(x|y) \ \mu(\text{d}x)=1$. For each $y\in \caly$ with $\pi(y)=0$, let $x\mapsto f(x|y)$ be some fixed (but arbitrary) measurable function on $\calx$ with $\int_\calx f(x|y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) =1$.

I claim that

\begin{equation}\label{plug-eqn} \int_A f(x|y)\pi(y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) = \int_A \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \end{equation}

for all $y\in \caly$ and all $A\in \mfm$. For proof, first note that if $\pi(y)\neq 0$, then this equation follows from the definition \eqref{cond-defn-eqn} given above. Otherwise, note that

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \leq \int_A \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \leq \int_\calx \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) = \pi(y), \end{equation}

so that the right-hand side of \eqref{plug-eqn} is $0$ if $\pi(y)=0$. This completes the proof of the claim.

Now, for each $y\in \caly$, define $A\mapsto K(A,y)$ to be the probability distribution on $(\calx,\mfm)$ with density $x\mapsto f(x|y)$ with respect to $\mu$. Then, given a product-measurable subset $E\subseteq \calx \times \caly$, we have

\begin{align} \int_\caly K(E(y),y) \ R(\text{d}y) &= \int_\caly \left\{ \int_{E(y)} f(x|y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \right\} \ R(\text{d}y) \notag \\ &= \int_\caly \left\{ \int_{E(y)} f(x|y)f_R(y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \right\} \ \nu(\text{d}y) \notag \\ &= \int_\caly \left\{ \int_{E(y)} \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) \right\} \ \nu(\text{d}y) \notag \\ &= \int_{\calx \times \caly} \varphi(x,y) I_{E(y)}(x) \ (\mu \times \nu)(\text{d}x, \text{d}y) \notag \\ &= \int_E \varphi(x,y) \ (\mu \times \nu)(\text{d}x,\text{d}y) \notag \\ &= Q(E) \notag, \end{align}

where the third equation follows from \eqref{plug-eqn}. This establishes the first disintegration formula \eqref{gen-disint-eqn}.

For each $y\in \caly$, we already know that $A\mapsto K(A,y)$ is a probability measure on $(\calx,\mfm)$. To complete the proof that $K$ is a stochastic transition kernel, we need to show that for each $A\in \mfm$, the function $y\mapsto K(A,y)$ is a measurable function on $(\caly,\mfn)$. If $\pi(y)\neq 0$, we have

\begin{equation}\label{almost-eqn} y\mapsto K(A,y) = \int_A f(x|y) \ \mu(\text{d}x) = \frac{\int_A \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x)}{\pi(y)}. \end{equation}

Since both $\pi$ and $y\mapsto \int_A \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x)$ are measurable functions, this shows that $y\mapsto K(A,y)$ is a measurable function on the measurable set $Z = \left\{ y \in \caly : \pi(y)\neq 0\right\}$. On the complement $Z^c$, the function $y\mapsto K(A,y)$ is constant, which is certainly measurable. Thus, $y\mapsto K(A,y)$ is measurable when restricted to both $Z$ and $Z^c$, and therefore it is measurable on all of $\caly$.

Finally, to prove the second disintegration formula \eqref{special-disint-eqn}, we apply \eqref{gen-disint-eqn} with $E = A\times B$, and observe that

\begin{equation}\notag K((A\times B)(y),y) = K(A,y)I_B(y) \end{equation}

for all $y\in \caly$. This completes the proof of the Measure Disintegration Theorem.

It will be convenient to extract the formulas for the densities used in the proof of the Measure Disintegration Theorem and place them in their own theorem:

Density Disintegration Theorem. Assume the notation and hypotheses of the Measure Disintegration Theorem. If $\varphi$ is a density of $Q$ with respect to $\mu\times \nu$, then $R$ has a density with respect to $\nu$ given by \begin{equation}\notag \pi(y) = \int_\calx \varphi(x,y) \ \mu(\text{d}x), \end{equation} while the probability measure $A\mapsto K(A,y)$ has a density with respect to $\mu$ given by \begin{equation}\notag x\mapsto f(x|y) = \frac{\varphi(x,y)}{\pi(y)} \end{equation} for all those $y\in \caly$ with $\pi(y)\neq 0$.
